# ARC-QUIC-RFC9002-0002 - QUIC RFC 9002 Recovery Ledger and Key-Discard Architecture

## Purpose

Describe the runtime-owned recovery ledger and key-discard cleanup slice for RFC 9002 Section 6.

## Scope

This design covers the runtime-owned recovery ledger per packet number space, selective key-discard cleanup for Initial, Handshake, and rejected 0-RTT packet-protection state, and the PTO reset path that follows early-key discard.
It keeps the sender/recovery state in the connection runtime and does not widen public API or TLS transport promises.

## Requirements Satisfied

- REQ-QUIC-RFC9002-S6-0001
- REQ-QUIC-RFC9002-S6P2P2-0005
- REQ-QUIC-RFC9002-S6P4-0001
- REQ-QUIC-RFC9002-S6P4-0002
- REQ-QUIC-RFC9002-S6P4-0003
- REQ-QUIC-RFC9002-S6P4-0004
- REQ-QUIC-RFC9002-SAP11-0003
- REQ-QUIC-RFC9002-SBP9-0001
- REQ-QUIC-RFC9002-SBP9-0002
- REQ-QUIC-RFC9002-SBP9-0003

## Design Summary

The connection runtime owns a per-packet-number-space sent-packet ledger and the send runtime tracks packet-protection level so discard can target the relevant recovery state instead of relying on helper-only math.
Initial and Handshake key discard continues to clear those spaces and reset PTO state, while rejected 0-RTT discard removes the zero-RTT sender ledger entries, queued retransmission plans, and associated bytes in flight without disturbing OneRtt traffic.

## Risks

- Selective 0-RTT cleanup must remove only sender-owned recovery state that belongs to rejected early data and must leave OneRtt packets intact.
- Initial and Handshake discard must still reset PTO behavior without widening the public surface or inventing a second sender ledger.
